title: Treacle scones, Scottish
subtitle: Dark, spiced griddle or oven scones
excerpt: Scottish treacle scones with black treacle, mixed spice and a touch of cinnamon. The treacle stains the crumb dark gold and gives a soft, slightly sticky texture. Twelve scones from the bowl in half an hour.
sourceNotes: F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929): the treacle-scone formula records the household ratio of black treacle to flour. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Scottish baking section notes the treacle scone as a tea-table standard. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the treacle handling (warm to loosen before mixing) appears in the spiced-cake section.

recipe.freezeNotes: Open-freeze unbaked scones on a tray; bag once solid; bake from frozen, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Warm the treacle and milk together the night before; chill the dry mix; combine on the morning.

recipeTools.notes: For warming the treacle and milk.

glossaryTerms.definition: Black treacle is stiff and viscous at room temperature and refuses to mix evenly with cold milk. Warming the treacle gently in a small pan with the milk until it loosens (a minute over low heat) lets the two combine into a thin, pourable liquid that incorporates evenly into the flour. Do not boil; the residual heat warms the dough and slows the bake.

content.text: Scottish treacle scones are a tea-table standard from the Lowlands and the East Coast. The treacle stains the crumb dark gold and gives a soft, slightly sticky texture; the mixed spice and cinnamon carry the flavour. The handling is the same as a plain scone (cold butter rubbed in, brief mixing) but the milk is replaced by a warmed treacle-and-milk mixture that loosens the treacle enough to incorporate.
Serve warm with butter. The treacle is sweet enough that jam is unnecessary.
Warm the treacle, rub in the butter
Warm the treacle and milk together

in a small pan over a low heat until the treacle loosens and the liquid is pourable. Set aside to cool to lukewarm. Sift the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{mixed-spice}} and {{cinnamon-ground}} into a bowl. Rub in the {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{soft-brown-sugar}}.

Pour the warm treacle-and-milk mixture into the flour all at once. Mix with a palette knife in figure-of-eight strokes until the dough comes together. The dough should look soft and a little tacky; if it is too dry add another tablespoon of milk. Tip onto a floured surface and pat to a 3 cm slab. Cut twelve rounds with a floured 5 cm cutter.

Set on a lined baking tray. Bake for 12–14 minutes; the tops should be deep mahogany (the treacle colours them quickly) and the sides should sound hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack 5 minutes before splitting.
